revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Anagiri Daijinja]][[ja:穴切大神社]] {{Expand Japanese|穴切大神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Anagiri Daijinja | native_name = 穴切大神社 | image = Anagiri shrine entrance.JPG | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Anagiri Daijinja | coordinates = {{coord|35.662638888888885|138.561}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Anagiri Daijinja'''|穴切大神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Kai Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Q134917287|ltq=Q134917287|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Q135026601|ltq=Q135026601|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Kai Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Kai Province|ja|甲斐国の式内社一覧|qid=Q11577868|1=List of Shikinaisha in Kai Province}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q201932|ltq=Q201932|qid=Q201932|1=Kōfu}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|35.662638888888885|138.561}} == image (P18) == * Anagiri shrine entrance.JPG == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Q134917316|ltq=Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|fa|بخشنامه شینتو|fr|Directive shinto|id|Pengarahan Shinto|ja|神道指令|zh|神道指令|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == heritage designation (P1435) == * {{ill|Q1188622|ltq=Q1188622|qid=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/840 ** P518: {{ill|Q2044994|ltq=Q2044994|qid=Q2044994|1=Honden}} ** Start time: {'time': '+1935-05-13T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} == country (P17) == * ᥓᥣᥱ ᥙᥣᥢᥱ|tet|Japaun|te|జపాన్|tg|Ҷопон|th|ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|thnews|หมวดหมู่:ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|tig|ጃፓን|ti|ጃፓን|tk|Ýaponiýa|tl|Hapon|tly|Japonijə|tn|Japan|tok|ma Nijon|to|Siapani|tpi|Siapan|trv|Tanah Tunux|tr|Japonya|trnews|Kategori:Japonya|trquote|Japonya|trvoyage|Japonya|ts|Japani|tt|Япония|tum|Japan|tw|Japan|tyv|Япония|ty|Tāpōnē|udm|Япония|ug|ياپونىيە|uk|Японія|uknews|Категорія:Японія|ukquote|Японія|ukvoyage|Японія|ur|جاپان|uz|Yaponiya|vec|Japon|vep|Japonii|ve|Japan|vi|Nhật Bản|vibooks|Các quốc gia/Nhật Bản|vivoyage|Nhật Bản|vls|Japan|vo|Yapän|war|Hapon|wa|Djapon|wo|Sapoŋ|wuu|日本|xal|Ниxуудин Нутг|xh|IJapani|xmf|იაპონია|yi|יאפאן|yo|Japan|za|Nyiedbonj|zea|Japan|zgh|ⵏⵉⴱⵓⵏ|zh_classical|日本|zh_min_nan|Ji̍t-pún|zh_yue|日本|zh|日本|zhnews|Category:日本|zhquote|日本|zhvoyage|日本|zu|IJaphani|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * あなぎりだいじんじゃ == postal code (P281) == * 400-0034 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5090005000701 == Commons category (P373) == * Anagiri Daijinja == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Kurotonano Shrine|lt=Kurotonano Shrine|qid=Q135039688|1=Kurotonano Shrine}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '山梨県甲府市宝二丁目8-5', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Anagiri Daijinja}} [[JA:穴切大神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11597204}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Anagiri Ōjinja |Image = [[File:Anagiri shrine entrance.JPG|270px]]
Approach| |Location = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|qid=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kōfu City|Q201932|qid=Q201932|1=Kōfu}} Takara 2-chōme 8-1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|qid=Q3275687}}
{{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} disputed shrine; former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded = Traditionally {{ill|lt=Wadō era|Q1373026|qid=Q1373026|1=Wadō (era)}} (early 8th century) |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with cypress-bark roofing |Other names = |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = April 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Anagiri Ōjinja|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Anagiri Ōjinja''' (あなぎりだいじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Takara 2-chōme, {{ill|lt=Kōfu City|Q201932|qid=Q201932|1=Kōfu}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|qid=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is associated with the lake-water legend and the “Kekiretsu” (mountain-splitting) legend of the {{ill|lt=Kōfu Basin|Q11577819|qid=Q11577819|1=Kōfu Basin}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. The shrine stands in the northern part of the Kōfu Basin, at the western edge of Kōfu’s central area, on the left bank of the {{ill|lt=Aikawa River (Yamanashi)|UNKNOWN|lt=Aikawa}} flowing north–south through the western part of the city. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines three deities: {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} (Ōkuninushi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|qid=Q3275687}}, and {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}. Until the early modern period it was commonly called “Anagiri Myōjin,” and in connection with the lake-water legend there is a theory that the original deity was Anagiri Myōjin or possibly Kekiretsu Myōjin.Saitō, “Anagiri Ōjinja.” === History === According to the *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kaikoku Shaki–Jiki|1=UNKNOWN}}* (hereafter *Shaki*), the shrine was originally called Kurotona Shrine, but later received the divine title “Anagiri Daimyōjin” from the {{ill|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|Q30253177|qid=Q30253177|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. It identifies the shrine as the *shikinaisha* “Kurotona Shrine” listed in the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}*, and the *{{ill|lt=Kai Kokushi|Q11577871|qid=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}* (hereafter *Kokushi*), vol. 55, Shrine Section I, also introduces the Kurotona Shrine theory citing the *Shaki*. However, as discussed below, the location of the shrine in the era of the *Jinmyōchō* appears to have belonged to {{ill|lt=Koma District|Q11479803|qid=Q11479803|1=Koma District}}.The *Kokushi* itself identifies the Kurotona Shrine as a *shikinaisha* with Kurotona Shrine (Karato Myōjin) in Kurashina Village (present-day Kurashina, Makioka Town, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi City|Q840610|qid=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}), and there is also a theory identifying it with Kurotona Shrine (Kurobe Myōjin) in Kurodaira Village (present-day Kurodaira Town, Kōfu City). These two shrines are regarded as leading candidates as disputed shrines (*ronsha*) (see *Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku*, vol. 10, Kōgakkan University Press, 1981). Both works present as the shrine’s origin the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Province Lake-Water Legend|1=UNKNOWN}}, also known as the Kekiretsu legend, in which the Kōfu Basin was once a great lake that was drained by cutting through mountains. According to the *Shaki*, during the {{ill|lt=Wadō era|Q1373026|qid=Q1373026|1=Wadō (era)}} (early 8th century), the provincial governor of {{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|qid=Q858076|1=Kai Province}} proposed converting the lake into arable land, petitioned the Imperial Court, and prayed to Ōkuninushi (Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto), a god of nation-building. He then cut through the basin’s southern area at Kajikazawa-guchi ({{ill|lt=Fujikawa Town|Q1351723|qid=Q1351723|1=Fujikawa, Yamanashi}}), drained the water into the {{ill|lt=Fuji River|Q596110|qid=Q596110|1=Fuji River}}, and carried out a major project to turn the basin into rice paddies. Upon completion, Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto, who had granted divine aid, was ceremonially enshrined as the shrine’s founding deity. The provincial governor who led the development was also worshiped as Kekiretsu Myōjin in the Kawauchi region along the Fuji River. It is also said that the regional name “Kawauchi” derives from “kawaochi” (river falling).*Yamanashi Encyclopedia (Revised and Expanded Edition)*, “Anagiri Ōjinja” (Yamanashi Nichinichi Shimbun, 1989). On the other hand, the *Kokushi* records a different tradition: that three deities drained the basin’s lake—first one kicked through the mountain ridge, the next bored a hole through the mountain to release the water, and the last guided the water to the Fuji River to form river channels. The first deity was called Kekiretsu Myōjin, the second Anagiri Myōjin, and the last Setachi Fudō. Citing an “old record,” it states that this shrine enshrines the second deity and that the name “Anagiri” derives from this act, concluding that “those who achieved flood-control works in ancient times were enshrined and revered as gods and Buddhas.” Throughout {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|qid=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, many legends remain that the basin was once a lake and was developed through divine or Buddhist power. Geologically, the Kōfu Basin is thought to have once been an inlet of {{ill|lt=Suruga Bay|Q1201023|qid=Q1201023|1=Suruga Bay}}, later shaped into its present form through {{ill|lt=folding|Q81659|qid=Q81659|1=Fold (geology)}} and volcanic activity. In the early modern period, the area including the shrine site—then part of Nishi-Aonuma VillageThe village area corresponds to present-day Takara 1–2 chōme, Marunouchi 2–3 chōme, Kotobuki-chō, and Aioi 1–2 chōme.—is considered a place-name remnant of Aonuma-gō in Koma District listed in the *{{ill|lt=Wamyō Ruijushō|Q2702542|qid=Q2702542|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}*. The name “Aonuma” is also said to derive from the many lakes and marshes that once dotted the area. Although it is difficult to accept at face value the *Shaki*’s claim that the basin was still a great lake in the Wadō era, archaeological evidence shows long habitation: the Enokida site in Chizuka, Kōfu City, is a settlement site from the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|qid=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}} through the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}}; the surrounding area contains the Chizuka–Yamamiya kofun group; the late-6th-century {{ill|lt=Kamunazuka Kofun|Q11398722|qid=Q11398722|1=Kamunazuka Kofun}} exists nearby; and the mid-6th-century {{ill|lt=Manjumori Kofun|Q22120416|qid=Q22120416|1=Manjumori Kofun}} is located in Yumura, Kōfu City. Thus, the area is thought to have been developed since the Yayoi and Kofun periods.*Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 19: Yamanashi Prefecture* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1984). {{ill|lt=Jōmon pottery|Q4390366|qid=Q4390366|1=Jōmon pottery}} has also been excavated near the shrine, suggesting ancient ritual activity, and some associate the standing stone of just over 1 {{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|qid=Q11573|1=Metre}} behind the main hall with the shrine’s founding. The earliest firm documentary reference to the shrine is a copy of a joint-signed certificate by the {{ill|lt=Four Magistrates of Tokugawa|Q11490263|qid=Q11490263|1=Four Magistrates of Tokugawa}} dated {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|qid=Q6735|1=1603}}), granting 5 koku and 7 to of black-seal shrine land in Nishi-Aonuma Village. The *Kokushi* records the shrine land as 5 koku and 2 shō, and this holding was maintained throughout the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. With the formation of the Kōfu castle town centered on {{ill|lt=Kōfu Castle|Q969909|qid=Q969909|1=Maizuru Castle Park}} in the Edo period, areas including parts of present-day Takara 1–2 chōme and Marunouchi 2–3 chōme, southwest of the castle, became outer samurai districts known as “Anagiri,” named after the shrine. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kami-Iida Daikansho|1=UNKNOWN}} magistrate’s office compound was also located there, and the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōfu Josui|1=UNKNOWN}} waterworks drawn from the Aikawa River ran through the area. A castle gate in Hyakuseki-chō was called “Anagiri Gate” after the shrine, and the shrine enjoyed the reverence of the warrior class, including the {{ill|lt=Kōfu Kinban|Q11577779|qid=Q11577779|1=Kōfu Kinban}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|qid=Q7753|1=1872}}), the shrine was ranked as a *gōsha*. When hereditary succession of the {{ill|lt=chief priest|Q11453500|qid=Q11453500|1=Gūji}} was abolished by the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji government|1=UNKNOWN}} the following year, the post became vacant at times, during which ancient records were lost.*History of Yamanashi Prefecture: Cultural Properties Volume*. === Festivals and rites === The annual festival is held on {{ill|lt=April 19|Q2523|qid=Q2523|1=April 19}}. According to the *Kokushi*, in the early modern period the Masaki family, with residences in Shin-Aonuma VillageA village separated from Nishi-Aonuma Village, corresponding to present-day Takara 1-chōme and Marunouchi 2-chōme., held the hereditary position of {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|qid=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}}. === Shrine buildings === === Main hall === The date of construction of the main hall is unknown, but stylistically it is thought to be a reconstruction from the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Momoyama period|1=UNKNOWN}}.Unless otherwise noted, this section is based on *History of Yamanashi Prefecture: Cultural Properties Volume*. It is a relatively small {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} one-bay shrine. A raised veranda with balustrades runs along three sides of the front and sides; *wakishōji* are set behind the core, and a pair of wooden *karado* doors is fitted at the center of the front. The bracket complexes are projecting sets of linked triple blocks with true bracket arms, with protruding *kobushibana* supporting richly colored eave purlins; the *kibana* on the head tie beams show elements of the {{ill|lt=Zen style|Q3575224|qid=Q3575224|1=Zenshūyō}}. The {{ill|lt=cypress-bark roof|Q90373477|qid=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} has straight eave members at the front, showing curvature only at the eave tips, differing from medieval shrine styles that curve the entire eave. The {{ill|lt=kōhai|Q11415970|qid=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} (worship porch) uses vividly colored triple-block brackets; the {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|qid=Q124421222|1=kōryō}} connecting the porch columns is simple in form but boldly lacquered in red and black, conveying a sense of dignity. The porch columns are not connected to the core columns by rainbow beams, instead featuring only carved {{ill|lt=tabasami|Q124660898|qid=Q124660898|1=tabasami}}. From the {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|qid=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}}, repairs and reroofing are known to have been carried out in {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|qid=Q1146221|1=Jōkyō}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1687|Q7668|qid=Q7668|1=1687}}) and {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|qid=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1765|Q7665|qid=Q7665|1=1765}}). Despite its small scale, the hall retains Momoyama-period decorative characteristics in its details, particularly in the {{ill|lt=kaerumata|Q123117911|qid=Q123117911|1=kaerumata}} and other relief carvings, which show early forms of the detailed ornamentation that developed from the Momoyama period onward. The shape of the *kibana* on the rainbow beam connecting the porch columns resembles those of shrine buildings constructed by the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeda clan|1=UNKNOWN}} in the late {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|qid=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, drawing attention as an example sharing common architectural traits. In {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1935|Q18658|qid=Q18658|1=1935}}), it was designated a National Treasure (former classification) under the {{ill|lt=National Treasures Preservation Act|Q11420716|qid=Q11420716|1=National Treasures Preservation Law}}; with the enforcement of the {{ill|lt=Cultural Properties Protection Act|Q1188502|qid=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}} on August 29, 1950, it became an {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|qid=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. Three attached ridge tags (two from Jōkyō 4 and one from Meiwa 2) were additionally designated in 1962. === Other buildings === The worship hall is a reinforced-concrete {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|qid=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} structure with copper-sheet roofing, entered from the gable side. The former worship hall, built in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku (Japan)|Q1193848|qid=Q1193848|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1715|Q6884|qid=Q6884|1=1715}}), was donated to {{ill|lt=Fujitake Shrine (Nirasaki)|UNKNOWN|lt=Fujitake Shrine}} in Nirasaki City.*Yamanashi Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer* (Yamanashi Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, 1985). [[File:Anagiri Shrine Gate.JPG|thumb|200px|Zuijin Gate]] The Zuijin Gate is a two-story {{ill|lt=rōmon|Q97378762|qid=Q97378762|1=Rōmon}} with {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|qid=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} roofing, three bays wide with a single central opening. According to its ridge tag, it was built in {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|qid=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1794|Q7811|qid=Q7811|1=1794}}) by the master carpenter Takeshita Genzō of {{ill|lt=Shimoyama carpenters|Q11360815|qid=Q11360815|1=Shimoyama carpenters}}. The carvings were executed by {{ill|lt=Tatekawa Kashiōrō|Q65277448|qid=Q65277448|1=Tatekawa Kashiōrō}} (Tomimune), the first master of the {{ill|lt=Tatekawa school (sculpture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatekawa school}}. Featuring excellent carvings of plants and animals such as {{ill|lt=peonies|Q247777|qid=Q247777|1=Order of Saint Louis}}, young leaves, {{ill|lt=lions|Q140|qid=Q140|1=Lion}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=elephants|1=UNKNOWN}}, the gate particularly displays the school’s characteristics in the *shakujō*-carving beneath the rainbow beams and the “shells on waves” motif beneath the upper-story purlins. Both architecture and sculpture are outstanding, and as a valuable example of late-Edo-period gate architecture, it was designated a Kōfu City cultural property in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|qid=Q1988|1=2001}}).Kōfu City Board of Education, “Anagiri Ōjinja Zuijin Gate” (on-site explanatory sign, February 2003). The {{ill|lt=karahafu|Q977293|qid=Q977293|1=Karahafu}}-roofed kagura hall is thought to have been built slightly earlier than the Zuijin Gate, though the projecting wings of the roof appear to be later additions. The form of these projections has been noted as similar to the kagura hall of {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|qid=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}} (Lower Shrine, Akimiya), an Important Cultural Property, which was raised in {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1835|Q7597|qid=Q7597|1=1835}}). === Auxiliary shrines === In addition to Shinmei Shrine, Shiogama Shrine, and Dōso Shrine, there are several small shrines, including stone shrines dedicated to Inari Daimyōjin. === Cultural properties === (Designation category and date in parentheses) ;Important Cultural Property *Main Hall, with three ridge tags (Structure; designated May 13, 1935; attached items additionally designated June 21, 1962) ;Kōfu City Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Zuijin Gate, with three ridge tags (Structure; designated March 30, 2001) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Norio Saitō, “Anagiri Ōjinja,” in Ken’ichi Tanigawa (ed.), *Gods of Japan—Shrines and Sacred Sites*, Vol. 10 (Tōkai), Hakusuisha, 1987 * *Place Names of Yamanashi Prefecture* (*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei* 19), Heibonsha, 1995 * *History of Yamanashi Prefecture: Cultural Properties Volume*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999 {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Anagiri Daijinja]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Anagiri Daijinja]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:甲府市の重要文化財]] [[Category:甲府市の歴史]] [[Category:甲府市の建築物]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]]
